title: Microsoft NT/Windows 2000 Multi-User Computing
author: Aberdeen Group
abstract: Aberdeen Group's 1998/1999 report examines the emerging market for thin-client/server-centric multi-user architectures built on Microsoft NT and Windows 2000. The report argues that technological improvements in late 1997 and 1998 have made multi-user architectures viable competitive weapons for enterprise IS managers. It surveys IS managers already evaluating NT multi-user computing and profiles vendors supplying components for Windows 2000-based multi-user environments.
importance_rationale: Aberdeen was an early authoritative voice on thin-client computing at a pivotal moment; this report helped IS decision-makers evaluate a nascent but strategically important architecture before Windows 2000 shipped.
relevance_rationale: The TCO analysis framework and multi-user vs. client/server trade-off methodology remain instructive; specific vendor profiles and Windows 2000 details are historically dated.
prescience_rationale: Aberdeen's prediction that multi-user Windows NT architectures would become 'business-altering competitive weapons' proved broadly correct — terminal services and Citrix MetaFrame became mainstream enterprise technologies by 2000-2002.
